result:
I want to write about the moment when the story you are living suddenly feels too small for your skin.  
It happens quietly, the way a kettle begins to hum before it shrieks. One Tuesday you are stirring yogurt into your coffee because the milk has gone sour, and you notice the spoon is the same one you used to mix paint in the third grade. You saved it from the craft bin because it was shiny, because your mother said we don’t waste what still glints. You have carried it through four dorm rooms, two marriages, and a storage unit in Tulsa that smelled of cedar and mouse droppings. The spoon is still shiny; you are not. You feel the handle warm against your palm and realise you have been gripping it the way sailors grip a rope in old engravings—like something is about to pull you overboard.

That is the moment I’m talking about. The too-small story. The suit of skin you’ve outgrown without noticing the seams popping. You finish the coffee, rinse the spoon, and the day stretches like taffy until every hour sticks to your teeth. You try to remember the last time you tasted something you’d never tasted before. Not fusion tacos or single-origin chocolate, but the shock of a colour you don’t have a name for, the moment when the foreign word cracks open and you see the concept that was missing from your native sky. You can’t remember. You open the fridge again, as though the answer is behind the pickles.

We are told to trust the arc, trust the process, trust that the mountain only reveals itself to the climber. But what if you are not on the mountain? What if you are on a roundabout that spins so smoothly you mistake motion for ascent? You circle the same strip mall: the pharmacy, the vape shop, the place that sells teriyaki in Styrofoam clamshells. You know the clerk’s tattoos by heart; he knows you buy floss only when you are angry. You both pretend this intimacy is enough. It is not.

I want to write about the crime of lowering expectation until it fits beneath a low ceiling like a bonsai that forgot it was supposed to become a forest. Not the expectation others set for you—those you have long since weaponised or discarded—but the ones you once set for yourself when you still spelled future with capital letters wide as cathedral doors. You remember writing I will be brave on the inside of your sneaker in seventh grade, right next to the sizing numbers. The sneaker is gone; the sentence is not. It walks around in your head, limping.

Some people explode their lives on purpose: they leave the marriage, the job, the time zone. They buy the ticket to Marrakesh or Moose Jaw and post pictures of themselves grinning at the edge of the frame, daring the viewer to believe joy is simple. Others stay and varnish the cracking wood until it shines like shame. Both choices can be acts of courage; both can be anesthesia. The difference is whether you wake up mid-surgery.

I want to write about waking up mid-surgery. About the terror of feeling the blade already inside you while the room hums with easy listening. You want to scream, but the protocol says you must wait for the designated moment of revelation. So you count ceiling tiles. You play games: recite the names of every teacher you ever had, catalogue the lies you told and the truths you let suffocate. You remember the dog you abandoned when you moved across the country—how she watched the car until the corner swallowed your taillights. You said it was kindness to leave her with your ex, but kindness was only the label on the box where you packed the unbearable weight of your guilt. You never wrote her name again until now: Sage. Her tail was a flag of unconditional surrender. You surrendered her.

Language itself turns traitor. The word settle, for instance, once meant putting something to rest; now it means accepting less than you wanted. The phrase settle down used to sound like a lullaby; now it’s a choke collar. You flinch when people ask about five-year plans. You want to answer: I plan to stop wincing when I hear my own voicemail greeting. I plan to remember the dream where I spoke fluent river. I plan to forgive the version of me who flinched from happiness because it arrived wearing the wrong costume.

Memory is a trick staircase: step where you stepped yesterday and you fall through. Whole years vanish like that. Yet other hours stay brutally accessible—the minute you tasted juniper on a wind that carried church bells from a village you’d never visit, the night you pressed your forehead to a cold window and pretended the glass was a glacier and you were small enough to be preserved inside it. You can still feel the condensation bead on your skin like the universe’s sweat. Why do we keep the wrong things? Why does the brain curate its museum with such perverse curatorial taste?

I want to write about the rebellion of delight, how it arrives disguised as inconvenience. The storm that knocks out power and you find the last candle, the one scented with pine though Christmas was three months ago. You light it and suddenly the living room becomes a cave where you and the cat negotiate a truce. You stop checking headlines. You tell her the story of the people who believed stars were holes poked by a sky-mouse seeking cheese. The cat blinks, unimpressed, but for the length of the flame you are levitating above the roundabout. The power returns; the spell cracks. You want to smash the light switch, but you don’t. You store the memory like smuggled currency.

There is a mythology that claims if you can name the exact weight of your sadness, the scale tips and the sadness dissolves. You try: it is the weight of every book you meant to read stacked on your chest, plus the silence of a father who answered questions with proverbs, plus the smell of the elementary-school lunchroom on pizza day, plus the envelope you still haven’t opened because it says final notice in a font that looks like teeth. You say the total out loud. The number changes nothing. Sadness is not arithmetic; it is weather. The best you can do is learn to walk in it without asking permission.

I want to write about the day you decide to stop walking and start growing roots where you stand. Not because the soil is ideal, but because the effort of dragging your own ghost has curved your spine into a question mark. You plant the yogurt spoon. You plant the voicemail. You plant the envelope unopened, because even unopened it is seed enough. You plant the word juniper and the word Sage. You plant the dream of fluent river. You plant the colour you don’t have a name for, the one that cracked the sky the night you almost kissed the person you knew you shouldn’t. You plant the almost. You plant the shouldn’t. You plant the kiss anyway.

Nothing happens, which is the first sign that something is happening. The roundabout keeps spinning, but you are no longer the one tied to the center pole. You are the pole. You are the roundabout. You are the child who once believed courage meant jumping off the moving merry-go-round, and the adult who now knows it can also mean standing still while everything whips past. You wait. You wait so long the seasons forget their lines and start improvising. Snow on the lilac. Sunburn at Thanksgiving. You wait until the spoon you planted grows into a tree that bears fruit shaped like keys. You wait until sadness learns your scent and lies down at your feet like the dog you never forgot. You wait until the fruit falls, and the keys open nothing except the certainty that doors were always a metaphor you could set on fire.

When you finally walk forward, the story fits again. Not because the skin shrank, but because you stopped trying to wear yesterday’s size. You step onto the mountain that was never outside the roundabout; the mountain was the motion, the view, the willingness to keep revolving until you saw every angle of the same strip mall and realised it was also a meadow before it was paved, and a coral reef before it was a meadow, and stardust before it was a reef. You circle back to the pharmacy, the vape shop, the teriyaki counter. The clerk’s tattoos have faded; you mention them. He blushes, says he’s saving for a cover-up. You tell him tattoos are just footnotes in a language we haven’t finished writing. He gives you extra sauce, no charge.

You go home. You brew coffee. The milk has not soured. You stir it with a new spoon, shiny as possibility. You write on the inside of your shoe: I will be brave is still true, but now it is spelled in lowercase, soft as breath, roomy enough for your foot to grow.

prompt: Write freely about whatever you want for 1000 words.
